FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, Year 2 follow-up (2018–2020), N=9,429, ages 11–12

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis reporting multiple logistic regression on cyberbullying victimization and perpetration

FT_IC2.evidence: Cyberbullying alone (victimization and perpetration) without separately-measured smartphone or social media use variable. Although abstract mentions "total screen time, particularly on the internet and social media," the outcome is cyberbullying victimization/perpetration, not SM/phone use as IV or DV paired with individual-trait variable. The paper measures cyberbullying as outcome with demographic/SES predictors; SM is mentioned as associated but cyberbullying-only does not qualify per criteria §2.4.

notes: Paper examines correlates of cyberbullying victimization and perpetration among ABCD participants but does not meet FT-IC2 because cyberbullying alone (without separately measured SM/phone variable) does not qualify as a qualifying exposure.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study baseline data (2016–2018), N=10,755, ages 9–10 years

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis using multiple linear regression to estimate associations between sociodemographic factors and 6 contemporary screen time modalities

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed publication in The Journal of Pediatrics, 2022

FT_IC2.evidence: Paper analyzes associations between sociodemographic factors (sex, race/ethnicity, country of birth, household income, parental education) and screen time modalities (TV, videos, gaming, social networking, texting, video chat). The "other side" of the relationship is demographic-only, not individual-trait-level. No individual-trait outcome (neural, behavioral, health, cognitive, well-being, sleep, academic, etc.) is paired with SM/phone use. Criteria §2.1 and §2.4 specify that demographic-only or contextual-only associations on the other side result in NOT_MET.

notes: Excellent contemporary screen time measurement across 6 modalities, but outcome variables are all sociodemographic (sex, race, income, parental education), not individual-trait-level health or behavioral outcomes.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, December 2020 COVID-19 rapid response survey, N=4,106, ages 12–14 years

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis using modified Poisson regression with coefficients transformed to risk ratios to estimate associations between COVID-19 information sources and masking behaviors

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed publication in Preventive Medicine Reports, 2022

FT_IC2.evidence: Analyzes exposure to COVID-19 information via social media (frequency of checking almost every hour or more) and television media (viewing an hour per day or more) in relation to masking behavior. Masking behavior ("I wear a mask over my face or protective gear") is an individual-trait-level behavioral outcome, not demographic or contextual. Meets criteria for qualifying SM/phone exposure paired with individual-trait-level variable.

FT_IC3.evidence: Inferential analysis with quantitative results: parent counseling on masking (adjusted risk ratio [ARR] 1.28, 95% CI 1.20–1.36), COVID-19 social media exposure (ARR 1.09, 95% CI 1.00–1.19), COVID-19 television media (ARR 1.09, 95% CI 1.01–1.17) all associated with 'always masking' in adjusted Poisson regression models

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple numeric results for SM/media↔outcome relationship: adjusted risk ratios with 95% confidence intervals reported in Table 2; unadjusted and adjusted models both presented

FT_EC7_reason: Design is cross-sectional (not longitudinal); FT-EC7 applies only to longitudinal designs where SM is DV-only. Paper does not meet the three simultaneous conditions required for FT-EC7.
notes: Strong fit to v6 criteria. Analyzes SM/media information exposure in relation to masking behavior (individual-trait outcome). Cross-sectional design. Meets all substantive criteria.
